topic: queen bee behaviour — hive has gone queenless

turns.content: I have a beekeeping concern and I should be grateful for your thoughts. I inspected one of my hives yesterday and I believe it has gone queenless. No eggs, no young larvae, and the bees seemed rather listless and disorganised. I am not sure of the best course of action at this time of year.

That does sound like a queenless hive. The absence of eggs and young larvae combined with the change in behaviour is a strong indicator. Before we discuss what to do, can I ask a few questions to get the full picture?

1. When did you last see eggs or the queen herself in this hive?
2. Did you see any queen cells — either emergency cells (on the face of the comb) or supercedure cells (usually on the lower edge)?
3. How strong is the colony in terms of numbers — are there still plenty of bees, or has it dwindled?
4. How many other hives do you have, and are they doing well?
5. Did you notice any signs of laying workers — scattered, domed cells with multiple eggs per cell?

The last time I saw eggs was about three weeks ago, at the previous inspection. The queen herself I have not seen for some time — she is not marked, which does not help. I did not find any queen cells, which is what concerns me. If they had made emergency cells, there would be something to work with, but there is nothing. The colony is still reasonably strong — perhaps three frames of bees — but noticeably less purposeful than my other hives. I have three others, all doing well. No sign of laying workers yet, thankfully.

Right, that's a clear picture, and the situation is manageable. Here's my assessment:

**The concern:** Three weeks without eggs and no queen cells means the colony has been queenless for a while and hasn't been able to raise a new queen on their own — probably because there were no eggs young enough to convert into queen cells by the time the queen was lost. The clock is ticking, because once a colony goes queenless for too long, laying workers can develop, and that's much harder to fix.

**The good news:** Three frames of bees is still a viable colony, and you have other strong hives to work with.

**Options at this time of year (late October):**

1. **Introduce a frame of eggs and young larvae from one of your strong hives.** This is the classic approach. Take a frame that has fresh eggs (less than three days old) and very young larvae. Place it in the queenless hive. If they're truly queenless, they should start building emergency queen cells within a day or two.
   - *However:* The timing is tricky. A new queen raised now won't be able to mate — there are very few drones flying in late October. She'd emerge unmated and be useless.

2. **Buy a mated queen.** This is the most reliable option at this time of year. A commercially reared, mated queen introduced in a cage will give the colony the best chance of surviving winter. Check with your local beekeeping association or suppliers — some still have queens available in October, though the season is getting late.

3. **Combine the queenless hive with one of your strong hives** using the newspaper method. This saves the bees and strengthens the receiving colony for winter. You lose the hive as a separate unit, but no bees are wasted.

Which of these appeals to you, or would you like me to go into more detail on any of them?

You have confirmed what I was thinking, I am afraid. The timing is the difficulty. Introducing a frame of eggs seems pointless if she cannot mate. I am not sure I can get hold of a mated queen this late — the suppliers I know of tend to stop around September. Which leaves combining.

I think combining is the right call at this stage. It's not a failure — it's sensible husbandry. Trying to nurse a queenless colony through winter without a laying queen is almost always a losing battle. The bees will dwindle, they'll consume stores without replacing themselves, and by February you'll likely find a dead colony.

Combining saves every bee and gives one of your other hives a welcome boost going into winter.

**The newspaper method:**

1. **Choose the receiving hive.** Pick your strongest colony — one that could benefit from the extra bees and stores.
2. **Remove the queenless hive's supers** and reduce it down to the frames with bees on them.
3. **Place a single sheet of newspaper** on top of the brood box of the receiving hive. Make a few small slits in the paper with your hive tool — just two or three, not too many.
4. **Place the queenless colony's frames** (in a box) directly on top of the newspaper.
5. **Leave them alone** for a few days. The bees will chew through the newspaper gradually, during which time the colony scents mingle and they accept each other peacefully.
6. After a week, you can go in, remove the newspaper remnants, and reorganise the frames as needed.

The whole process is remarkably gentle. Fighting is very rare with the newspaper method.

Would it help to talk through which of your three hives would be the best candidate to receive the bees?

I think my hive on the south side of the garden would be the best choice. It is the strongest but it could still use the extra foragers in the spring. The two out-apiary hives are rather too far away to carry frames to. A practical question — should I move the queenless hive next to the receiving hive first, or does it not matter?

Good question, and yes, it does matter — but at this time of year, less so than in summer. Here's the thinking:

**The issue:** If the queenless hive is currently some distance from the receiving hive, the foragers from the queenless colony will try to return to their original location after the combine. In summer, you'd lose a lot of bees this way.

**At this time of year:** Most of the bees are staying in the hive anyway — there's very little foraging happening in late October in the Cotswolds. So the risk of losing returning foragers is much smaller.

**My suggestion:**
- If the two hives are within about ten metres of each other in the garden, just go ahead and combine directly. The bees will sort themselves out.
- If they're further apart, you could move the queenless hive a metre or so per day toward the receiving hive over the course of a week, then combine. But honestly, at this time of year, I wouldn't lose sleep over it. The bees aren't flying much, and they'll orient to the new location quickly.

One other thing to consider: when you combine, the queenless colony's stores come with them. Make sure the combined hive isn't too heavy with honey — you don't want the bees clustering too far from the brood area in cold weather. If there's excess, you can remove a frame or two of capped honey and keep it as emergency feed for later in the winter.

Does that all make sense?

It does, and it is reassuring. The two hives are about eight metres apart, so I shall just get on with it. I must say, it is always a little sad to lose a colony as a separate entity. I have had that hive for three years. But the bees themselves carry on, which is the important thing.

That's exactly the right way to look at it. The colony as a unit is a temporary thing — queens are replaced, bees are born and die, swarms depart and new ones arrive. The individual bees from your queenless hive will go on living and contributing, just under a different roof. And in the spring, when the combined colony is bursting with vigour, you can split it and have your fourth hive back again if you wish.

Three years is a good run for a colony. You kept them healthy and productive, and now you're making the sound decision rather than the sentimental one. That's good beekeeping.

I'd suggest doing the combine on a mild day this week if you can — the bees will be calmer, and you'll be more comfortable working without gloves getting stiff in the cold. A still afternoon with some weak sun would be ideal.

Do come back and let me know how it goes.

I shall do it on Wednesday if the weather holds — there is meant to be a dry spell. Thank you for talking me through it. It is helpful to have one's instincts confirmed by someone who can lay it all out clearly.

Wednesday sounds perfect. And your instincts were spot on — you'd already diagnosed the problem and identified the likely solution before we started. Sometimes it just helps to hear it said aloud.

I hope it goes smoothly. A sheet of newspaper and a steady hand, and by next week you'll have one very strong hive ready for winter. Good luck, and do let me know how the combined colony settles in.
I shall. Thank you, as always. Good afternoon.
Good afternoon. Best of luck on Wednesday.
